flashcards.Back: A logical grouping of network devices that behave as if they are connected to the same physical switch, regardless of their location. it is used to segment broadcast domains.
A switch interface configured to carry traffic for only one specific VLAN. it is typically used to connect end-user devices like PCs and printers.
A VLAN configured to carry standard user-generated traffic such as web browsing and file transfers, isolated from voice or management traffic.
A specialized VLAN on an access port that allows an IP phone to send tagged voice traffic while a connected PC sends untagged data traffic.
VLAN IDs from 1 to 1005. These are the standard IDs used in most enterprise networks and are stored in the switch's vlan.dat file.
The pre-configured VLAN on a Cisco switch (VLAN 1) to which all ports are assigned by default out of the box.
A 12-bit number used to identify a specific virtual LAN. The IDs range from 1 to 4094, with 1-1005 being the normal range.
The Cisco IOS command that forces a switch port to function as a permanent access port, preventing it from negotiating a trunk link.
The interface subcommand used to assign a specific VLAN ID to an access port, ensuring the connected device resides in that broadcast domain.
The command used to define the VLAN ID for voice traffic on an access port, enabling the port to support an IP phone and a PC simultaneously.
The collection of VLAN configuration data stored in the vlan.dat file. it contains the IDs and names of all normal range VLANs defined on the switch.
The primary Cisco command used to verify VLAN configuration. it displays a list of all active VLANs and the access ports assigned to each one.
A logical division of a network where all nodes can reach each other by broadcast. VLANs are used to reduce the size of these domains.
The concept where a single VLAN exists across multiple physical switches, allowing members of the same logical group to communicate regardless of their physical port.
Standard Ethernet frames that do not contain a VLAN ID. In an access port configuration, all incoming and outgoing frames are untagged.
A network device that forwards frames based on MAC addresses. it can use VLANs to separate traffic but cannot route between them.
The ability for hosts in different VLANs to communicate. This requires a Layer 3 device like a router or multilayer switch to move traffic between domains.
The process of associating a physical switch port with a specific logical VLAN ID, effectively placing the connected device into a chosen network segment.
The configured state of a port, such as 'static access,' which defines its intended function regardless of the connected neighbor's settings.
The actual resulting state of a port (e.g., 'static access') after negotiation. For an access port, this should always match the administrative mode.
A configuration command ('shutdown' within VLAN mode) that administratively disables a specific VLAN, stopping all traffic forwarding for that ID.
An optional descriptive label assigned to a VLAN ID (e.g., 'Sales' or 'HR') to make the network configuration more human-readable.
The binary file in flash memory where normal range VLANs are stored. if this file is deleted, the switch loses its normal range VLAN definitions.
The principle where end devices are unaware of the VLANs they belong to, as the switch handles all logical segmentation transparently.
A port-based membership method where the administrator manually assigns each port to a VLAN. This is the most common method in CCNA-level networking.
A configuration that allows switches to synchronize VLAN information. if not used correctly, it can lead to accidental deletion of VLANs across the entire network.
A 100 Mbps switch port. Access ports on these interfaces are frequently used for connecting standard workstations and IP phones.
A 1000 Mbps switch port. modern access switches often use these for high-performance end-user connectivity and uplinks.
The requirement that a VLAN must be defined with the same ID on all switches where its members reside to ensure seamless communication across trunks.
A detailed Cisco command used to verify the administrative and operational modes of an interface, as well as its assigned access and voice VLANs.
